\chapter{Boolean Analysis — Bernoulli Cost}
%%% generated by the blueprint pipeline from TCSlib.BooleanAnalysis.LMN.BernoulliCost
\section{Overview}
This module compares the fixed-size restriction model $R_k$ (uniform over restrictions
with exactly $k$ free variables) with the Bernoulli restriction model $R_p$, and shows
that switching from $R_k$ to $R_p$ costs nothing asymptotically: if the bad event has
probability at most $(5kw/n)^s$ under every $R_k$, then under $R_p$ it has probability at
most $(10pw)^s$ plus an exponentially small tail $e^{-np/3}$ that vanishes as $n \to \infty$.

\section{Declarations}

\begin{definition}[Restrictions with a fixed number of free variables]
\lean{BernoulliCost.fixedSizeRestrs}
\leanok
\uses{SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.freeVars}
For natural numbers $n$ and $k$, this is the finite set of restrictions on $n$ variables
whose set of free variables has cardinality exactly $k$.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Fixed-size restriction probability $R_k$]
\lean{BernoulliCost.fixedSizeRestrProb}
\leanok
\uses{BernoulliCost.fixedSizeRestrs, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction}
The probability of an event under the fixed-size restriction model $R_k$, which is uniform
over restrictions with exactly $k$ free variables. It is defined as the fraction of
restrictions with exactly $k$ free variables that satisfy the event.
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Binomial probability mass function]
\lean{BernoulliCost.binomialPMF}
\leanok
The binomial probability mass function, $\Pr[\mathrm{Bin}(n,p) = k] = \binom{n}{k}\, p^k\, (1-p)^{n-k}$.
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}[Fixed-size restriction probability is nonnegative]
\lean{BernoulliCost.fixedSizeRestrProb_nonneg}
\leanok
\uses{BernoulliCost.fixedSizeRestrProb, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction}
For any event, the fixed-size restriction probability satisfies
$0 \le \mathrm{fixedSizeRestrProb}\,(\mathrm{event})\,k$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Fixed-size restriction probability is at most one]
\lean{BernoulliCost.fixedSizeRestrProb_le_one}
\leanok
\uses{BernoulliCost.fixedSizeRestrProb, BernoulliCost.fixedSizeRestrs, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction}
For any event, the fixed-size restriction probability satisfies
$\mathrm{fixedSizeRestrProb}\,(\mathrm{event})\,k \le 1$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Binomial PMF is nonnegative]
\lean{BernoulliCost.binomialPMF_nonneg}
\leanok
\uses{BernoulliCost.binomialPMF}
If $0 \le p \le 1$, then $0 \le \mathrm{binomialPMF}\,n\,p\,k$ for every $k$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Binomial PMF sums to one]
\lean{BernoulliCost.binomialPMF_sum_one}
\leanok
\uses{BernoulliCost.binomialPMF}
If $0 \le p \le 1$, then the binomial PMF sums to $1$ over $k$ ranging in $\{0, \dots, n\}$,
i.e.\ $\sum_{k=0}^{n} \mathrm{binomialPMF}\,n\,p\,k = 1$. This is the binomial theorem
applied to $(p + (1-p))^n = 1$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Decomposition of Bernoulli probability by free-variable count]
\lean{BernoulliCost.bernoulli_decompose}
\leanok
\uses{BernoulliCost.binomialPMF, BernoulliCost.fixedSizeRestrProb, BernoulliCost.fixedSizeRestrs, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction.freeVars, SwitchingLemma2.bernoulliRestrProb, SwitchingLemma2.bernoulliRestrWeight}
For $0 \le p \le 1$ and any event, the Bernoulli$(p)$ restriction probability decomposes
according to the number of free variables:
\[
  \Pr_{R_p}[\mathrm{event}] = \sum_{k=0}^{n} \mathrm{binomialPMF}\,n\,p\,k \cdot \mathrm{fixedSizeRestrProb}\,(\mathrm{event})\,k,
\]
because conditioning $R_p$ on having exactly $k$ free variables yields exactly the model $R_k$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{lemma}[Chernoff upper-tail bound for the binomial distribution]
\lean{BernoulliCost.chernoff_binomial_upper_tail}
\leanok
\uses{BernoulliCost.binomialPMF}
For $0 < p \le 1$, the upper tail of the binomial distribution beyond $2np$ is exponentially
small: summing $\mathrm{binomialPMF}\,n\,p\,k$ over those $k \in \{0, \dots, n\}$ with $k > 2np$
is at most $e^{-np/3}$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{theorem}[Bernoulli restriction cost, exact version]
\lean{BernoulliCost.bernoulli_restriction_cost}
\leanok
\uses{BernoulliCost.bernoulli_decompose, BernoulliCost.binomialPMF, BernoulliCost.binomialPMF_nonneg, BernoulliCost.binomialPMF_sum_one, BernoulliCost.chernoff_binomial_upper_tail, BernoulliCost.fixedSizeRestrProb, BernoulliCost.fixedSizeRestrProb_le_one, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.bernoulliRestrProb}
Let $n > 0$, $0 < p \le 1$, and $w, s > 0$. If the event satisfies
$\mathrm{fixedSizeRestrProb}\,(\mathrm{event})\,k \le (5kw/n)^s$ for every $k \le n$, then under
the Bernoulli model,
\[
  \Pr_{R_p}[\mathrm{event}] \le (10pw)^s + e^{-np/3}.
\]
\end{theorem}

\begin{lemma}[Eventual smallness of the exponential tail]
\lean{BernoulliCost.exp_neg_eventually_small}
\leanok
For any $p > 0$ and any $\varepsilon > 0$, there exists $N$ such that for all $m \ge N$ one has
$e^{-mp/3} < \varepsilon$; that is, the tail term decays to $0$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{theorem}[Bernoulli restriction cost, asymptotic version]
\lean{BernoulliCost.bernoulli_restriction_asymptotic}
\leanok
\uses{BernoulliCost.bernoulli_restriction_cost, BernoulliCost.exp_neg_eventually_small, BernoulliCost.fixedSizeRestrProb, SwitchingLemma2.Restriction, SwitchingLemma2.bernoulliRestrProb}
Let $0 < p \le 1$, $w, s > 0$, and $\varepsilon > 0$. Then there exists $N$ such that for every
$n \ge N$ with $n > 0$ and every event satisfying
$\mathrm{fixedSizeRestrProb}\,(\mathrm{event})\,k \le (5kw/n)^s$ for all $k \le n$, one has
$\Pr_{R_p}[\mathrm{event}] \le (10pw)^s + \varepsilon$. Asymptotically the exponential tail
vanishes, leaving only the leading bound $(10pw)^s$.
\end{theorem}
